name: Occupant Modern Text
classification_notes: Explicitly placed within the Didone or modern classification by its designer; it adapts the high-contrast, delicate details of that tradition for text use rather than display.
summary: A text-optimised Didone by Cyrus Highsmith, adapted from Occupant Modern with compact spacing and refined details suited to body copy in print and on screen.
description: Derived from Cyrus Highsmith's Occupant Modern, this text companion retools the display family's delicate proportions and tight spacing to hold up at smaller sizes without losing the characteristic high contrast of the Didone tradition. Marie Otsuka led the italic design and assisted with production, contributing five weights from Extra Light to Bold, each with a matching italic. Coverage extends across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with small caps, oldstyle figures and tabular figures rounding out a thorough typographic system.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Modern Text is part of Cyrus Highsmith's exploration into the Didone or modern classification of typefaces. He adapted the delicate details, dimensions and compact spacing of Occupant Modern to work at text sizes in print as well as on screen, with Marie Otsuka driving the design of the italics and assisting with production.

name: Occupant Modern
classification_notes: Explicitly classified as Didone or modern by the foundry itself; high contrast between thick and thin strokes with vertical stress, tracing a lineage from Bodoni through Japanese Mincho typography.
summary: A Didone family by Cyrus Highsmith tracing an unusual lineage from Bodoni through Ikko Tanaka's high-contrast Mincho typeface Kocho, with italics developed by Marie Otsuka.
description: The design draws a cross-cultural line from 18th-century Italian punchcutting through 20th-century Japanese graphic design, using Tanaka's Kocho as an intermediate reference rather than going directly to Bodoni. Thirty styles span five weights across upright and italic cuts in standard, condensed and compressed widths. Coverage extends to Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, with small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and localized forms throughout.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Modern is Cyrus Highsmith's exploration of the Didone or modern classification, inspired by Kocho, a high-contrast Mincho-style typeface by Japanese graphic designer Ikko Tanaka, who himself drew inspiration from Giambattista Bodoni. Marie Otsuka led the design of the italics and assisted with production.

name: Carp
classification_notes: Carp is a humanist sans-serif taking structural cues from Excoffon's Antique Olive, with a distinctive top-heavy stroke contrast that becomes more pronounced in heavier weights and relaxes toward a monolinear form in lighter ones.
summary: A humanist sans by Marie Otsuka built on Antique Olive's top-heavy stroke logic, stretched across a variable family spanning three widths and six weights.
description: Taking Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive as its structural starting point, Carp inverts the conventional weight distribution so that tops of letterforms carry more mass than the bottoms. This asymmetry sharpens visibly in heavier weights while lighter styles settle into a near-monolinear register, making the family pull double duty across display and body text. Variable axes for width and weight run across Condensed, Standard and Wide subfamilies, with full small caps, oldstyle figures and coverage of Latin Extended, Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Carp as Marie Otsuka's streamlined take on the humanist sans-serif genre, inspired by Excoffon's Antique Olive and its characteristic top-heavy stroke balance. The asymmetric contrast is exaggerated in heavier styles while lighter cuts lean toward a functional monolinear sans, with variable axes across width and weight yielding a versatile family suited to both expressive display and body text use.

name: Allium Rounded
classification_notes: Derived from Cyrus Highsmith's humanist sans serif Allium, with rounded stroke terminals added by Marie Otsuka; the humanist proportions and letterform structure remain the defining characteristic.
summary: A rounded adaptation of Cyrus Highsmith's Allium, reworked by Marie Otsuka with softened stroke terminals and adjusted proportions for headline and display use.
description: Marie Otsuka extended Highsmith's Allium series by rounding the stroke endings and refining the proportions to suit the new character. The family runs across five weights from Extra Light to Black, each with an italic, and covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek scripts. It is positioned for headline and display sizes where the softened terminals read clearly without becoming novelty.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium Rounded as grown from Cyrus Highsmith's humanist sans series Allium by Marie Otsuka, who rounded the stroke endings and adjusted the original proportions to suit the new design. They note it works best at headline and display sizes.

name: Allium
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans serif by the foundry; the open, calligraphic letterforms and pan-European character set are consistent with humanist sans conventions.
summary: A calm humanist sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn for headlines and deck sizes with broad pan-European script coverage.
description: Designed in 2017 by Cyrus Highsmith, Allium spans six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek. The family is intended for headline and deck use, and its quietly restrained character makes it well suited to editorial and cultural contexts. The series also extends into Allium Text and Allium Rounded, offering distinct voices within the same design logic.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium as a humanist sans serif with a pan-European character set, drawn by Cyrus Highsmith for headlines and deck sizes. They characterise it as calm, even quiet, and note that Highsmith named it after the genus of flowering plants that includes onions, leeks and garlic.

name: Allium Text
classification_notes: Allium Text is a humanist serif engineered for small text sizes, with accentuated stroke contrast and adjusted proportions that place it firmly in the text-optimised humanist serif tradition.
summary: A text-optimised cut of Cyrus Highsmith's Allium, tuned for the 8 to 24-point range with accentuated stroke contrast and recalibrated spacing.
description: Highsmith reduced the Allium family to its essentials for sustained reading, adjusting proportions and spacing to suit smaller sizes while increasing the contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms to extend legibility. Eight styles run from Light to Black with matching italics. Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek coverage makes it a practical choice for multilingual publishing and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith engineered Allium Text as a reduced, optimised version of the Allium family, designed specifically to perform in the 8 to 24-point range. Beyond resizing proportions and spacing, he accentuated the contrast between the interior and exterior of letters to increase their broadcast range, with assistance from June Shin and Cem Eskinazi.

name: Allium Rounded
classification_notes: Derived from Allium, a humanist sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith, with rounded stroke terminals added by Marie Otsuka. The humanist proportions and organic rhythm are retained from the parent family.
summary: A rounded companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Allium, developed by Marie Otsuka with softened stroke terminals and adjusted proportions suited to headline and display use.
description: Marie Otsuka derived Allium Rounded from the humanist sans series Allium by Cyrus Highsmith, rounding the stroke endings and refining the proportions to complement the new texture. Ten styles span five weights from Extra Light to Black, each with a matching italic. The character set covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, with oldstyle figures, tabular figures and a range of stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium Rounded as grown from Cyrus Highsmith's humanist sans serif series Allium by Marie Otsuka, who rounded the stroke endings and adapted the original proportions to suit the new design. They recommend it for headline and display sizes.

name: Allium Text
classification_notes: Allium Text is a humanist serif optimised for small sizes, with accentuated stroke contrast and adjusted proportions that point to a classical humanist tradition adapted for text-setting performance.
summary: A text-optimised derivative of Cyrus Highsmith's Allium family, redrawn with tighter proportions and accentuated stroke contrast for legibility in the 8 to 24-point range.
description: Highsmith engineered this cut specifically for text sizes, adjusting spacing, proportions and the relationship between the inside and outside of letterforms to extend their broadcast range at small sizes. Four weights from Light to Black each carry an italic, giving the family enough range for structured editorial hierarchies. Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek coverage broadens its reach across multilingual publishing.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith distilled the Allium family into a text version calibrated for 8 to 24 points, reworking proportions, spacing and stroke contrast to increase legibility at size. June Shin and Cem Eskinazi assisted in its development.

name: Allium
classification_notes: Highsmith describes Allium explicitly as a humanist sans serif, and the broad pan-European character set, open apertures and calligraphic underpinning confirm that classification.
summary: A calm humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, built for headlines and deck sizes with a wide pan-European character set covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2017, Allium draws on humanist sans conventions and keeps its tone deliberately restrained, making it well suited to headline and deck-level setting rather than extended body text. Twelve styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics throughout, and the character set spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek scripts. The family extends into Allium Text and Allium Rounded, broadening its range of applications.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium as a humanist sans serif with a pan-European character set, designed by Cyrus Highsmith. They characterise it as calm, even quiet, drawn for headlines and deck sizes. The name comes from the genus of flowering plants that includes onions, leeks and garlic.

name: Amira 2
classification_notes: Amira sits between Optima and Lydian, combining classical sans structure with calligraphic energy through angled stress and distinctive rhythm. It has no serifs yet carries strong calligraphic character, placing it firmly in the calligraphic sans tradition.
summary: A calligraphic sans by Cyrus Highsmith occupying the rare territory between Optima and Lydian, updated in 2023 with Greek, Cyrillic and small caps.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith and expanded by Cem Eskinazi in 2023, Amira 2 is a letterform that fuses classical sans structure with angled calligraphic vitality, producing a texture on the page that is both readable and distinctly alive. The family spans five weights from Light to Black, each with italics, and the 2023 revision added small caps alongside Greek and Cyrillic coverage. A companion Amira Mono family extends its character into monospaced settings.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira as occupying rare ground between Hermann Zapf's Optima and Warren Chappell's Lydian, combining calligraphic spirit with classical sans structure. Originally designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2004, the face was expanded in 2023 by Cem Eskinazi to include small caps and Greek and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Amira Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface derived from Amira, itself a sans with calligraphic spirit sitting between Optima and Lydian. The fixed-width mechanical rhythm imposed on organic calligraphic forms places it firmly in the mono category with an unusual humanist character.
summary: A monospaced adaptation of Cyrus Highsmith's calligraphic sans Amira, redrawn in 2025 by Cem Eskinazi to impose a fixed-width rhythm on its organic, angled letterforms.
description: Where Amira sits in the rare territory between Optima's calligraphic sans and Lydian's unseriffed calligraphy, Amira Mono takes that organic vitality and locks it to a mechanical grid. Cem Eskinazi's adaptation produces a typographic texture that is genuinely unusual: the tension between calligraphic stroke contrast and monospaced regularity gives it a distinct personality absent from most coding or terminal typefaces. Ten styles run from Light to Black with italics throughout, and coverage extends across Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira Mono as the result of Cem Eskinazi imposing a mechanical beat on Amira's organic forms, taking Cyrus Highsmith's original letterform and pushing it further into a unique typographic texture. They frame the original Amira as occupying rare ground between Zapf's Optima and Chappell's Lydian, with Amira Mono extending that territory into fixed-width type.

name: Amira Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface derived from Amira, itself a calligraphic sans in the tradition of Optima and Lydian; the fixed-width constraint sits over organic, angled calligraphic forms, making it a rare fusion of monospaced mechanics and calligraphic structure.
summary: A monospaced extension of Cyrus Highsmith's Amira, redrawn by Cem Eskinazi in 2025 to impose fixed-width mechanics on letterforms rooted in calligraphic sans tradition.
description: Where Amira sits between Optima's classical sans structure and Lydian's calligraphic spirit, Amira Mono takes those organic, angled forms and disciplines them into a mechanical, fixed-width cadence. Cem Eskinazi extended the family in 2025 with ten styles across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic. Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek coverage, alongside small caps, oldstyle figures and a full suite of OpenType features, gives it broad typographic range for both screen and print work.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira Mono as Cem Eskinazi imposing a mechanical beat on Amira's organic calligraphic forms, creating a unique typographic texture. They position the original Amira, by Cyrus Highsmith, within rare territory between Optima and Lydian, and Amira Mono as pushing even further into that territory.

name: Antenna Serif
classification_notes: A slab companion to the sans-serif Antenna, sharing its structural DNA while adding bracketed or unbracketed serifs across a wide range of weights and widths; its systematic family architecture is characteristic of contemporary workhorse slabs.
summary: A 56-style slab companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, developed with David Jonathan Ross across seven weights and four widths with full italics.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2015 and developed with David Jonathan Ross, the family spans seven weights from Thin to Black across four widths: standard, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed, each with matching italics. The range gives it genuine utility across print and digital contexts wherever a structured, space-efficient slab is needed. Its systematic pairing relationship with Antenna makes it a natural choice for multi-family editorial systems.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna Serif as the slab counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, developed with David Jonathan Ross and weighing in at 56 styles across seven weights in four widths, each with italics, suitable for both print and digital formats.

name: Antenna 2
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an industrial sans serif; Highsmith shifted design emphasis to the white counterforms inside and between letters, giving it a deliberate, structured character distinct from humanist or geometric traditions.
summary: A seven-weight, four-width industrial sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith, updated in 2025 with extended language support and variable fonts.
description: Originally released in 2006, Antenna 2 was built around the pattern of white shapes formed inside and between characters, with Highsmith deliberately slowing his typical fast curves to achieve a more measured, confident feel. The 2025 revision, led by Victoria Rushton, adds broader language coverage across Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, as well as variable font support. Fifty-six styles span seven weights from Thin to Black across Regular, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed widths, all with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna 2 as a versatile industrial sans serif that projects confidence and deliberation. Cyrus Highsmith slowed his usual fast curves and shifted focus to the pattern of white shapes inside and between characters. The 2025 release, led by Victoria Rushton, expands language support and introduces variable fonts to a family already offering seven weights across four widths with matching italics.

name: Amira 2
classification_notes: Amira sits between calligraphic and sans-serif traditions, drawing comparisons to Optima and Lydian in its stress, angled strokes and letterform energy without serifs. The foundry frames it as occupying rare territory between Hermann Zapf's Optima and Warren Chappell's Lydian.
summary: A calligraphic sans by Cyrus Highsmith that draws on the traditions of Optima and Lydian, expanded in 2023 with small caps, Greek and Cyrillic by Cem Eskinazi.
description: Combining classical sans structure with the angled energy of calligraphic letterforms, Amira 2 occupies a narrow genre between Optima's refined flare and Lydian's unseriffed pen strokes. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, and the 2023 expansion by Cem Eskinazi added small caps alongside Greek and Cyrillic script support. The result is a text family with distinctive rhythm and texture, suited to literary, editorial and multilingual typesetting.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira as inhabiting rare territory between Hermann Zapf's Optima and Warren Chappell's Lydian, a letterform that pops from the page with angled vitality and brings bright new rhythms to readers. The 2023 update by Cem Eskinazi added small caps and extended the character set to include Greek and Cyrillic.

name: Antenna
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an industrial sans serif; Highsmith's design emphasises the pattern of white counter-shapes, giving it a deliberate, structured quality distinct from humanist or geometric traditions.
summary: An industrial sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith built around the rhythm of internal white shapes, offered in seven weights across four widths with matching italics.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2006, Antenna slows his characteristic fast curves and redirects attention to the pattern of counters and inter-character spacing. The result is a measured, self-assured typeface that holds up across a wide range of settings. With 56 styles spanning Regular, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed widths, the family covers everything from text columns to tight display headlines.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna as a versatile industrial sans serif that projects confidence and deliberation. Highsmith slowed his usual fast curves and shifted the emphasis to the pattern of white shapes formed inside and between characters. Seven weights in four widths with matching italics make it ready for almost anything.

name: Antenna Serif
classification_notes: Antenna Serif is the slab-serif companion to Highsmith's Antenna grotesque, sharing its proportional structure across four widths with bracketed slab serifs rather than sans terminals.
summary: A 56-style slab companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, developed with David Jonathan Ross across seven weights and four widths with italics throughout.
description: Developed by Cyrus Highsmith with David Jonathan Ross, this slab serif mirrors the structure of the Antenna grotesque family, extending it into serif territory across Regular, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed widths. Each of the seven weights carries a matching italic, giving the full family 56 styles and considerable range for complex typographic systems. Suited to print and digital work where a slab counterpart to a sans family is needed without tonal mismatch.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna Serif as the slab counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, developed with David Jonathan Ross, weighing in at 56 styles across seven weights in four widths, each with italics, and playing well in both print and digital formats.

name: Antenna 2
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an industrial sans serif; the design emphasises the pattern of white counter and inter-character spaces over conventional stroke modulation, placing it in a neutral but deliberate grotesque-adjacent tradition.
summary: A deliberately paced industrial sans by Cyrus Highsmith, updated in 2025 with Victoria Rushton, spanning seven weights across four widths with full italic complements and variable font support.
description: Originally released in 2006, Antenna 2 was drawn with slowed curves and an emphasis on the white shapes formed inside and between letters rather than the strokes themselves, lending it a calm, deliberate character unusual among workhorse sans families. The 2025 revision adds expanded language support across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, plus variable fonts, making the 56-style family considerably more deployable. Its four widths from Regular to Compressed give it practical range across display, editorial and UI contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna 2 as a versatile industrial sans serif projecting confidence and deliberation. Cyrus Highsmith slowed his usual fast curves and concentrated on the pattern of white shapes formed inside and between characters. Originally released in 2006, the 2025 update was led by Victoria Rushton and adds broader language support and variable fonts to the existing seven weights in four widths with matching italics.

name: Biscotti
classification_notes: Biscotti is based on traditional English roundhand calligraphy, simplified for 21st-century typographic use. Its origins in copperplate-influenced script place it firmly in the script category with a roundhand subcategory.
summary: A simplified take on English roundhand calligraphy, drawn by Cyrus Highsmith in 2004 for headline and pull-quote use in Brides magazine.
description: Drawn in 2004 for Brides magazine, Biscotti translates traditional English roundhand calligraphy into a clean, contemporary script suited to display headlines and pull quotes. Two weights, Regular and Bold, carry extended Latin character support including accented forms and standard ligatures. The letterforms are deliberately simplified rather than strictly imitative, giving the face a lighter editorial quality without the fussiness of historical copperplate revival.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Biscotti as Cyrus Highsmith's response to the prompt 'I want a typeface that makes me feel pretty.' Originally drawn in 2004 for Brides magazine to set headlines and pull quotes, it is a simplified 21st-century interpretation of traditional English roundhand calligraphy.

name: Daleys Gothic
classification_notes: Drawn with a steel brush and ink, Daley's Gothic is a compact display grotesque with a handmade quality suited to headlines, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A three-weight display grotesque drawn with a steel brush by Cyrus Highsmith, designed for compact headlines and named after his mother.
description: Begun in 1997 with a steel brush and ink on paper, the family was refined through multiple drawn variations before settling on three weights, Regular, Bold and Black, each with matching italics. The brush origins give it a warmth and slight irregularity that distinguishes it from more mechanical grotesques, making it well suited to editorial headline work. The character set includes ligatures, stylistic alternates and extended Latin diacritics.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts note that Cyrus Highsmith began Daley's Gothic in 1997, experimenting with a steel brush and ink on paper. After drawing many variations, he settled in 1998 on three weights with matching italics, intended for compact headlines, and named the family after his mother, who taught him how to draw.

name: Carp
classification_notes: Carp is a humanist sans-serif drawing on Excoffon's Antique Olive tradition, with an inverted stress where letterform tops carry more weight than the bases, a characteristic of the humanist sans genre rather than grotesque or geometric conventions.
summary: A humanist sans by Marie Otsuka with inverted stress drawn from Excoffon's Antique Olive, available as a variable font spanning weight and width axes across condensed, standard and wide subfamilies.
description: Designed by Marie Otsuka and released in 2025, Carp takes the top-heavy asymmetric contrast of Antique Olive as its starting point and pushes that weight imbalance further in the heavier styles, while the lighter cuts settle into a near-monolinear rhythm. Variable axes across weight and width span three subfamilies, Condensed, Regular and Wide, each running from Extra Light to Black with matching italics. Small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and extensive language support across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek round out a family that works at both display and text sizes.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Carp as Marie Otsuka's streamlined take on the humanist sans-serif genre, inspired by Excoffon's Antique Olive, in which the tops of letters are heavier than the bottom parts. In Carp, this asymmetric weight balance and contrast is exaggerated in the heavier styles, while the lighter styles lean toward a more functional monolinear sans. With variable axes across width and weight, the result is a versatile family suited to both expressive display and body text use.

name: Dispatch Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced adaptation of a slab serif, Dispatch Mono retains the industrial slab structure of its parent family while enforcing fixed-width spacing, placing it firmly in the monospaced category with slab serif characteristics.
summary: A monospaced slab serif drawn by Cyrus Highsmith from his own Dispatch family, originally made for shipping labels and printing PostScript code.
description: Adapted from Highsmith's industrial slab serif Dispatch, this monospaced variant was first made for personal use before being made commercially available. It comes in Regular and Bold with corresponding italics, covering an extended Latin character set with broad diacritic support. The origins in functional, industrial use give it a plainness that suits code editors, data display and utilitarian editorial work alike.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Dispatch Mono as a monospaced version of Cyrus Highsmith's industrial strength slab serif, Dispatch, originally made for his own use on shipping labels and printing PostScript code, now available to everyone in regular and bold weights with corresponding italics.

name: Dispatch Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of a slab serif family, with the fixed-width constraint built in from the outset for coding and label printing use cases.
summary: A monospaced slab serif drawn by Cyrus Highsmith from his own Dispatch typeface, originally made for shipping labels and PostScript code.
description: Derived from Highsmith's industrial-strength Dispatch, this fixed-width cut keeps the slab serif character of the original while fitting each glyph to a uniform advance width. The family offers four styles across regular and bold weights with matching italics, covering an extended Latin character set. Its utilitarian origins in shipping labels and code printouts give it a practical, no-frills personality suited to terminal interfaces, technical documentation and monospaced editorial use.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Dispatch Mono as a monospaced version of Cyrus Highsmith's industrial strength slab serif Dispatch, originally made for his personal use on shipping labels and printing PostScript code, now available to everyone in regular and bold weights with corresponding italics.

name: Dispatch 2
classification_notes: Highsmith draws on typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels to produce a taut, utilitarian slab serif suitable for both text and display work. The uniform stroke weight and squared serifs align it closely with industrial or Egyptian slab traditions.
summary: A slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith drawn from the letterforms of typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels, spanning five widths and five weights with matching italics.
description: Created by Cyrus Highsmith and re-released in 2023 as Dispatch 2 with assistance from Cem Eskinazi, the family pulls its character from industrial and commercial sources rather than typographic convention. Fifty styles cover five widths from Compressed to Wide and five weights from Extra Light to Bold, all with italics, giving it unusual range for both text setting and headline work. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek are covered, along with a full suite of stylistic alternates and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Dispatch 2 as an industrial-grade slab serif designed by Cyrus Highsmith for text and display use, drawing on letterforms found in typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels. Cem Eskinazi assisted with its re-release. The foundry suggest it is built for heavy commercial lifting.

name: Eggwhite
classification_notes: Eggwhite is a display typeface defined by unconventional weight distribution across two playful weights, making it unsuitable for text use and firmly in the display category with a novelty character.
summary: A two-weight display typeface by Cyrus Highsmith built around unconventional weight distribution, conceived as a sketchbook doodle during a meeting.
description: Cyrus Highsmith sketched the idea for Eggwhite during a meeting in 1999, releasing it as an experiment in redistributing stroke weight in ways that defy convention. The family comprises just two styles, Lite and Black, each carrying the same eccentric character. Its limited weight range and playful construction make it a typeface suited to display settings where personality outweighs neutrality.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Eggwhite as born from Cyrus Highsmith's sketchbook during a meeting, an experiment in unconventional weight distribution that comes in a pair of playful weights: Black and Lite.

name: Gasket
classification_notes: Gasket is a humanist sans with a slight slant and subtly staggered stroke endings that give it an informal, animated rhythm, placing it clearly in the humanist sans tradition despite its playful character.
summary: A warmly animated humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, originating as a single companion font for his children's book illustrations.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2016, Gasket grew from a single bespoke font into a full family of six weights with matching italics and small caps. A gentle hop in rhythm comes from a slight slant and subtly staggered stroke endings, giving text an amiable, informal energy without sacrificing legibility. The family extends into Gasket Unicase and Gasket Uncial, broadening its expressive range.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket as beginning as a single font Cyrus Highsmith drew to accompany his illustrations in a children's book. The amiable series moves with a gentle hop produced by a slight slant and subtly staggered endings; weights, italics, and small caps were added for its wider release.

name: Dispatch 2
classification_notes: Highsmith describes Dispatch as an industrial-grade slab serif drawing on typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels, placing it firmly in the utilitarian slab tradition with a pronounced mechanical character.
summary: A wide-ranging industrial slab designed by Cyrus Highsmith, drawing on typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels for text and display use.
description: Cyrus Highsmith built Dispatch around letterforms found in utilitarian sources: typewritten documents, engineering drawings and freight labels. The re-release as Dispatch 2, with assistance from Cem Eskinazi, spans five width variants from Compressed to Wide, five weights from Extra Light to Bold, and matching italics throughout, giving it an unusually broad palette for demanding commercial work. Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek coverage make it workable across a wide range of publishing and identity contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant describe Dispatch 2 as an industrial-grade slab serif created by Cyrus Highsmith for text and display, assembled from the letterforms of typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels. Cem Eskinazi assisted with its re-release, and Occupant recommend it for heavy commercial lifting.

name: Gasket Uncial
classification_notes: Gasket Uncial is derived directly from the uncial calligraphic script tradition dating to the fourth century, with particular reference to insular manuscripts such as the Book of Kells. Its letterforms blend majuscule and minuscule characteristics in the manner typical of uncial scripts, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A contemporary interpretation of the uncial script tradition by Cyrus Highsmith, drawing on Irish insular manuscripts including the Book of Kells.
description: Highsmith roots this family in the uncial calligraphic tradition stretching back to the fourth century, with the Book of Kells as a touchstone. Six weights from Thin to Bold each carry an italic, giving the family unexpected range for a script-derived design. The result sits between historical reference and usable text display, avoiding the costume-dress quality that typically afflicts revival uncials.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Uncial as Cyrus Highsmith's update of ancient uncial calligraphy, citing the Book of Kells as the most famous example of the script and noting that Highsmith hopes his Irish ancestors would be proud of the result.

name: Gasket Unicase
classification_notes: Gasket Unicase is a display typeface built around a unicase system where oversized lowercase forms are drawn to match the height of capitals, eliminating ascenders and descenders entirely. This structural conceit places it firmly in the display category with a distinct unicase subcategory.
summary: A unicase display family by Cyrus Highsmith in which oversized lowercase letters are drawn to sit flush with the capitals, removing ascenders and descenders entirely.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith in 2016, Gasket Unicase resolves the upper/lower case decision by designing lowercase forms large enough to occupy the same visual band as capitals. With no ascenders or descenders, the resulting text block sits in an unusually even horizontal register. Six weights from Thin to Bold, each with an italic, give the family range for display and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Unicase as a solution for the busy typographer who cannot decide which case to use. Cyrus Highsmith designed it with extra-large lowercase forms that align with the capitals, and the family contains no ascenders or descenders.

name: Gasket Uncial
classification_notes: Gasket Uncial is drawn from the uncial script tradition, a historical calligraphic hand associated with early medieval manuscripts such as the Book of Kells; its display character and historical roots place it firmly in the display category with uncial as its specific subcategory.
summary: Cyrus Highsmith's revival of the uncial script tradition, tracing a lineage from fourth-century calligraphy through the Book of Kells into a contemporary six-weight family.
description: Rooted in the uncial hand that shaped early medieval manuscripts, Gasket Uncial translates that ancient calligraphic tradition into a type family of six weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic. Highsmith designed it with personal connection, citing his Irish ancestry as a motivating thread. The family sits alongside Gasket and Gasket Unicase within the broader Gasket system, and its extended Latin character set makes it serviceable for a range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Uncial as Cyrus Highsmith's update of uncial calligraphy, a script dating to the fourth century and most famously exemplified in the Book of Kells, circa 800 CE. Highsmith brought personal investment to the project, hoping his Irish ancestors would approve of the result.

name: Gasket Unicase
classification_notes: Gasket Unicase is a unicase display design with oversized lowercase letterforms sized to match the capitals, eliminating ascenders and descenders entirely. Its uncial-influenced construction and deliberate case-blending place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A unicase display family by Cyrus Highsmith in which oversized lowercase letters are drawn to sit flush with the capitals, removing all ascenders and descenders.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2016, Gasket Unicase solves the upper-versus-lower case dilemma by making both cases share the same visual height. The absence of ascenders and descenders produces an unusually even, ribbon-like texture across any line of text. Six weights from Thin to Bold, each with an italic, give the family a useful range for display and titling work.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Unicase as designed for the busy typographer who cannot decide which case to use. Cyrus Highsmith drew extra-large lowercase forms to match the capitals, and the result contains no ascenders or descenders.

name: Heron Sans
classification_notes: Mechanically inspired letterforms with warmth and energy suggest a humanist sans approach; the design balances technical rigour with organic character, a hallmark of humanist sans-serif tradition.
summary: A mechanically inspired sans-serif by Cyrus Highsmith, designed to work across both headline and text settings with equal reliability.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2008, Heron Sans draws on mechanical forms while introducing warmth and energy that keep it from feeling cold or clinical. The family spans five weights from Light to Bold, each with italics, and extends into a full Condensed subfamily for tighter column widths and display work. Its extended Latin character set and broad stylistic alternates make it a practical choice for editorial and multilingual publishing.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Sans as steadfast and no-nonsense, always ready to go to work. Cyrus Highsmith galvanised its mechanically inspired letters with warmth and energy, resulting in a versatile family capable of performing in both headlines and text settings.

name: Gasket
classification_notes: Gasket is a humanist sans with a slight slant and subtly staggered stroke endings that give it an informal, hand-influenced rhythm, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: Originally drawn by Cyrus Highsmith to accompany his own children's book illustrations, Gasket has grown into a six-weight family with italics and small caps.
description: A humanist sans with a gentle, loping quality produced by a subtle slant and slightly staggered stroke endings. Highsmith expanded it from a single bespoke font into a full family of twelve styles running from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, plus comprehensive small caps and stylistic alternates for double-storey a and g. Related sub-families Gasket Unicase and Gasket Uncial extend its character further.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts note that Cyrus Highsmith began Gasket as a single font to accompany his illustrations in a children's book, and that the amiable series moves with a gentle hop thanks to its slight slant and subtly staggered endings. He subsequently added weights, italics and small caps before releasing it publicly.

name: Heron Serif
classification_notes: Heron Serif is explicitly described as the slab serif counterpart to Heron Sans, featuring bracketed slabs and a versatile range suited to both text and display work.
summary: A slab serif companion to Heron Sans by Cyrus Highsmith, covering five weights with condensed cuts and Greek and Cyrillic alphabets added by Cem Eskinazi.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2010 as the slab serif counterpart to his Heron Sans, this family runs five weights from Light to Bold with matching italics, plus a full Condensed subfamily. Cem Eskinazi extended the character set to include Greek and Cyrillic, making it a strong choice for multilingual publishing. The design holds its own at both text sizes and in headlines, giving it broad utility across print and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Serif as the slab serif counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Heron Sans, noting it as a versatile design capable of performing in both headlines and text. Greek and Cyrillic alphabets were contributed by Cem Eskinazi.

name: Heron Sans
classification_notes: Mechanically inspired letterforms with warmth and energy, typical of humanist sans design; broad weight and width range with strong text and display versatility.
summary: A mechanically inspired humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, built for reliable performance across both headline and text settings.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2008, Heron Sans pairs mechanical letterform construction with humanist warmth across five weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic. A full Condensed subfamily mirrors the weight range, adding flexibility for tighter measures and display work. Broad Latin Extended character support and a generous suite of alternates, ligatures and figure sets make it well suited to editorial and text-heavy environments.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Sans as steadfast and no-nonsense, always ready to go to work. Cyrus Highsmith galvanised its mechanically inspired letters with warmth and energy, producing a versatile family capable of performing at both headline and text sizes.

name: Heron Serif
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a slab serif counterpart to Heron Sans, with bracketed slabs visible across its letterforms, spanning both text and display use.
summary: A slab serif companion to Heron Sans by Cyrus Highsmith, covering five weights with condensed counterparts and extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2010 as the slab serif pendant to Heron Sans, the family runs across five weights from Light to Bold, each with an italic, and is mirrored by a full Condensed subfamily. Cem Eskinazi extended the character set to cover Greek and Cyrillic, making it a capable choice for multilingual publishing. The design works at both headline and text sizes without requiring a separate optical cut.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Serif as the slab serif counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Heron Sans, calling it a versatile design capable of performing in both headlines and text. Greek and Cyrillic alphabets were added by Cem Eskinazi.

name: Ibis Text
classification_notes: Ibis Text draws on Walbaum and Melior as source references, combining classical transitional structure with the oval, geometric forms associated with Melior, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition adapted for text use.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith drawing on Walbaum and Melior, originally designed for his own typography textbook.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2008, Ibis Text fuses the classical proportions of Walbaum with the oval geometry of Zapf's Melior to produce a family tuned for sustained reading. It was drawn specifically for Highsmith's typography textbook Inside Paragraphs, giving it a practical grounding in long-form typesetting. Six weights from Extra Light to Black are supplied with matching italics and small caps throughout.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Ibis Text as the product of combining two unlikely references: Walbaum's 1919 types and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. Cyrus Highsmith drew it for use in his own typography textbook, Inside Paragraphs, and the family covers a wide range of weights alongside small caps and corresponding italics.

name: Ibis Text
classification_notes: Ibis Text draws on Walbaum and Melior as sources, combining sharp transitional contrast with slightly squared oval forms. The blend of 18th-century rational structure and mid-century geometric influence places it firmly in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith drawn originally for his own typography textbook, fusing the contrast of Walbaum with the slightly squared ovals of Melior.
description: Designed in 2008 for use in Highsmith's textbook Inside Paragraphs, Ibis Text combines the sharp weight contrast of Walbaum with the subtly geometric forms of Zapf's Melior. Six weights from Extra Light to Black are each accompanied by a matching italic, and full small caps are included across the range. The result is a text family with enough personality for editorial use and enough structure to hold up in sustained reading.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith drew Ibis Text by combining two unlikely references: Walbaum's 1919 typeface and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. Originally designed for use in his own typography textbook Inside Paragraphs, the family offers a full range of weights, small caps and corresponding italics.

name: Ibis Display
classification_notes: Ibis Display draws on Walbaum and Melior as dual references, blending a transitional serif structure with the rationalist oval forms associated with Melior's superellipse geometry. The display cut features thinned serifs and tighter fitting suited to larger sizes.
summary: A display serif by Cyrus Highsmith that crosses Walbaum's sharp contrast with Melior's oval geometry, offered across three widths and six weights with italics throughout.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2009, Ibis Display takes Walbaum's sharp-contrasted letterforms and tempers them with the rationalist oval construction of Zapf's Melior. For the display cut, serifs are thinned and spacing tightened to suit headline sizes, producing a typeface with presence and precision at large scale. Three width families, standard, Condensed and Compressed, each span six weights from Extra Light to Black with corresponding italics, giving editors and designers considerable range within a single family.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith drew inspiration for the Ibis series from an unlikely pairing: Justus Erich Walbaum's 1919 Walbaum and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. For the display version, he thinned the serifs and tightened the fitting, resulting in a family with a wide range of weights and widths, each accompanied by italics.

name: Icebox
classification_notes: Icebox is a modular sans serif designed for headlines and short text, with letterforms derived from the geometric shapes of plastic magnetic letters, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn from the shapes of plastic magnetic refrigerator letters, available in three weights plus a companion Magnet style.
description: Highsmith designed Icebox in 2012 after years of keeping a box of magnetic letters he bought from a variety store in 1997. The letterforms are built from the rounded, uniform geometry of those colourful plastic tiles, giving the family a playful rigidity suited to headlines and short copy. Three weights from Light to Bold are joined by the bonus Icebox Magnet style, with Latin Extended character coverage throughout.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Icebox as a modular sans serif for headlines and short blurbs, inspired by a box of magnetic plastic letters Cyrus Highsmith bought from a variety store in 1997. Available in three weights with a bonus Magnet style, it requires no refrigeration.

name: Icebox Magnet
classification_notes: A modular sans serif built from discrete geometric units, inspired by plastic magnetic refrigerator letters, designed explicitly for headlines and short text rather than body copy.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, inspired by plastic magnetic refrigerator letters, available in three weights plus a novelty Magnet style.
description: Designed in 2012 and rooted in a box of magnetic plastic letters Highsmith picked up in 1997, Icebox Magnet is built on a modular grid that gives each letterform a chunky, tile-like quality. Three standard weights are joined by a Magnet variant that leans further into the toy-letter conceit. Coverage extends across Latin Extended, making it usable across a broad range of Western European languages despite its display-only intent.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Icebox Magnet as a modular sans serif for headlines and short blurbs of text, available in three weights plus a bonus Magnet style. The design was inspired by a box of colourful plastic magnetic letters that Cyrus Highsmith bought from a variety store in 1997, and it requires no refrigeration.

name: Ibis Display
classification_notes: Ibis Display draws on Walbaum and Melior as its twin sources, blending transitional and humanist serif qualities. The display cut features thinned serifs and tighter fitting, placing it firmly in the high-contrast transitional serif tradition adapted for display use.
summary: A display serif by Cyrus Highsmith that fuses the sharpness of Walbaum with the rationalist curves of Melior across six weights and three widths.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2009, Ibis Display synthesises Justus Erich Walbaum's 1919 Walbaum and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior into a high-contrast display serif with thinned serifs and compact letter-spacing. The family spans six weights from Extra Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, each with corresponding italics, giving editors and designers a broad palette for headlines and titling. Small caps, multiple numeral styles, ligatures and stylistic alternates add typographic depth throughout.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith drew inspiration for the Ibis series by combining Justus Erich Walbaum's 1919 Walbaum and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. For the display version, he thinned the serifs and tightened the fitting, resulting in a wide range of weights and widths with corresponding italics.

name: Loupot
classification_notes: Loupot is derived from a calligraphic logotype and exhibits both industrial and calligraphic qualities, functioning as an upright script capable of all-caps setting — an unusual property that sets it apart from conventional cursive scripts.
summary: An upright script expanded by Cyrus Highsmith from Charles Loupot's 1938 St. Raphaël aperitif logotype, one of the few scripts that works convincingly in all-caps settings.
description: Originally a logotype drawn by French designer Charles Loupot in 1938 to promote St. Raphaël aperitif wine, the design was later expanded into a typeface by Cyrus Highsmith in collaboration with New York designer and illustrator Laurie Rosenwald. The result sits in an unusual position between industrial construction and calligraphic fluidity. Its ability to hold together in all-caps makes it genuinely versatile for display and branding applications where most scripts would falter.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Loupot as at once industrial and calligraphic, rooted in Charles Loupot's celebrated 1938 St. Raphaël logotype. Laurie Rosenwald brought the concept to Cyrus Highsmith, who developed it into one of the few scripts capable of a successful all-caps setting.

name: Icebox Magnet
classification_notes: Icebox Magnet is a modular sans serif built from geometric, uniform-stroke shapes inspired by plastic magnetic letters, making it firmly a display face suited to headlines rather than continuous text.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, inspired by the plastic magnetic refrigerator letters he bought in 1997, available in three weights plus a bonus Magnet style.
description: Built from uniform, geometric modules that echo the chunky plastic shapes of fridge magnets, Icebox is designed for headlines and short text rather than extended reading. Three weights are accompanied by a Magnet style, which presumably pushes the magnetic-letter concept further. Despite its playful origins, the Latin Extended character set is thorough enough for broad Western European use.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith bought a box of magnetic letters from a variety store in 1997, and the colorful plastic shapes eventually inspired Icebox. Released in 2012, it is a modular sans serif for headlines and short blurbs, offered in three weights plus a bonus Magnet style. Refrigeration not required.

name: Icebox
classification_notes: Icebox is a modular sans serif designed explicitly for headlines and short text, with letterforms derived from the geometric shapes of plastic magnetic letters, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn from the plastic shapes of refrigerator magnet letters he bought in 1997.
description: Inspired by a box of colourful magnetic letters purchased from a variety store, Cyrus Highsmith built Icebox around a modular, geometric construction suited to headlines and short runs of text. Three weights span Light to Bold, with a bonus Magnet style rounding out the family. The extended Latin character set covers a broad range of accented characters, making it more capable than its playful origins might suggest.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Icebox as a modular sans serif for headlines and short blurbs, available in three weights plus a bonus Magnet style. The design was inspired by a box of magnetic plastic letters Cyrus Highsmith bought from a variety store in 1997, and they note, with some levity, that it requires no refrigeration.

name: Mantar
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a Scotch Roman, with the psychedelic and phototypesetting influences giving it a soft, display-oriented character with curvy and elongated terminals.
summary: A Scotch Roman display face rooted in phototypesetting specimens, with soft curves, elongated endings and a distinctly psychedelic warmth across six weights.
description: Designed by Cem Eskinazi and inspired by a phototypesetting specimen, Mantar reinterprets the Scotch Roman tradition through a soft, display-oriented lens. Its curvy letterforms and elongated stroke endings give it an organic, slightly trippy character that sits apart from more rigid historical revivals. Twelve styles span Extra Light through to an expressive Ultra, each with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Mantar as a psychedelic Scotch Roman by Cem Eskinazi, conceived after he encountered a phototypesetting specimen and was drawn to the warmth of its letters. The result is a soft display face with curvy, elongated endings, available in 12 styles including a funky Ultra weight.

name: Magmatic
classification_notes: A utilitarian sans-serif with a nuts-and-bolts character loosely inspired by Japanese Showa-era industrial typefaces, sitting closer to gothic or grotesque traditions than humanist or geometric schools.
summary: A broad sans-serif family by Cyrus Highsmith and June Shin, spanning six weights across five widths and drawing loose inspiration from Japanese Showa-era industrial lettering.
description: Thirty styles run across Compressed, Condensed, standard, Extended and Wide widths, each offered in six weights from Thin to Black, giving the family genuine range for complex typographic systems. The design source is Japanese Showa-era lettering found on shipping labels, machinery and buildings, and that utilitarian, nuts-and-bolts character persists consistently through every width and weight. Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek script coverage makes the family viable for multilingual publishing and branding.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Magmatic as a massive sans-serif series designed by Cyrus Highsmith and June Shin, with a nuts-and-bolts character that holds steady across an extensive range of widths and weights. The design was loosely inspired by Japanese Showa-era typefaces found on shipping labels, machinery and buildings. Released in 2019.

name: Occupant Gothic
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a frantic angular design with no clear function, originating from Highsmith's caffeinated sketching style; it does not fit neatly into conventional grotesque or blackletter categories and is best placed as an expressive display face.
summary: A frantic angular sans from Cyrus Highsmith, originating in his caffeinated sketchbook sessions of the late 1990s, available in two weights with italics.
description: Born from Highsmith's personal sketching style circa 1998, this typeface makes no pretence at utility and wears that proudly. The letterforms are angular and irregular in a way that reads as energetic rather than crude, carrying the idiosyncratic character of a designer's working drawings translated directly into type. Two weights, Regular and Bold, each with matching italics, make for a small but purposeful family suited to expressive display contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Occupant Gothic as a typographic embodiment of Cyrus Highsmith's caffeinated sketching style from around 1998, calling it a frantic angular design with no clear function. The name predates the founding of Occupant Fonts and is noted to sound particularly good in Highsmith's Wisconsin accent.

name: Occupant Modern Text
classification_notes: Explicitly classified by the foundry as Didone or modern; high contrast strokes with compact spacing adapted for text use, following the classical vertical stress and thin-to-thick construction of the Didone tradition.
summary: A text-optimised Didone by Cyrus Highsmith and Marie Otsuka, adapted from Occupant Modern for reliable performance at small sizes in print and on screen.
description: Derived from Occupant Modern, this family condenses the delicate details and tight spacing of the Didone classification into a form that holds at text sizes. Five weights from Extra Light to Bold each carry a matching italic, with the italics driven by Marie Otsuka. Script coverage spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, and the glyph set includes small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Modern Text is part of Cyrus Highsmith's exploration into the Didone or modern classification of typefaces. He adapted the delicate details, dimensions and compact spacing of Occupant Modern to work at text sizes in print and on screen, with Marie Otsuka driving the design of the italics and assisting with production.

name: Occupant Modern
classification_notes: Explicitly classified as Didone or modern, featuring high contrast between thick and thin strokes characteristic of the style, inspired by Bodoni via Ikko Tanaka's Mincho-style Kocho.
summary: A high-contrast Didone by Cyrus Highsmith that traces a line from Bodoni through Ikko Tanaka's Mincho-style Kocho, with italics co-designed by Marie Otsuka.
description: Drawing on an unexpected cross-cultural lineage, the design connects 18th-century Italian punchcutting to 20th-century Japanese graphic design, producing a Didone with a quietly distinctive character. Thirty styles span five weights from Extra Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, each with a matching italic. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage, alongside small caps, oldstyle figures and tabular figures, makes it unusually complete for a display-oriented serif.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Modern is described as Cyrus Highsmith's exploration of the Didone or modern classification, inspired by Kocho, a high-contrast Mincho-style typeface by Japanese graphic designer Ikko Tanaka, who in turn drew inspiration from 18th-century Italian punchcutter Giambattista Bodoni. Marie Otsuka drove the design of the italics and assisted with production. Released in 2025.

name: Occupant Oldstyle
classification_notes: An oldstyle text serif distinguished by rounded triangular endings on strokes, drawing from Japanese Mincho type traditions alongside Western oldstyle conventions, resulting in a hybrid that sits firmly in the oldstyle serif category despite its cross-cultural influences.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith and June Shin that fuses oldstyle conventions with rounded triangular stroke endings drawn from Japanese Mincho type, rounded sans serifs and assorted pop-cultural sources.
description: Highsmith describes the distinctive terminals as 'child-safe serifs', a phrase that captures both their softened geometry and the long, eclectic development process behind them. Five weights from Extra Light to Bold each carry a matching italic, and the family covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek scripts with small caps throughout. The cross-cultural reference points give the face an idiosyncratic warmth that sets it apart from standard oldstyle text revivals.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Occupant Oldstyle as a 21st century text face designed by Cyrus Highsmith with assistance from June Shin, released in 2021. They note that its rounded triangular endings emerged from a long fermentation process combining ingredients from Japanese Mincho typefaces, rounded sans serifs, electronic music and toys, with Highsmith coining the term 'child-safe serifs' for the resulting details.

name: Prensa Display
classification_notes: A display-optimised serif with sharper features and tighter fit than its text companion Prensa, designed for headline use across standard, condensed and compressed widths.
summary: A display companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Prensa text family, sharpened and tightened for powerful headlines across three widths.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith as a headline companion to his Prensa text family, Prensa Display retains the distinctive internal tension of the original while sharpening its features and narrowing its fit. Thirty-six styles span six weights from Light to Black with italics throughout, and the family extends into Condensed and Compressed widths for demanding editorial layouts. Small caps, oldstyle figures and extensive Latin Extended coverage round out a family built for serious editorial and news environments.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa Display as a companion to Highsmith's popular Prensa text face, sharing its distinctive letterform tension but with sharper features, a more compact fit, and additional narrow widths suited to packing in powerful headlines.

name: Pentameter
classification_notes: Pentameter is a monospaced typeface with uniform character widths throughout; its defining characteristic is an "upright italic" construction where letterforms carry an italic rhythm while maintaining monospaced metrics.
summary: A monospaced typeface by Marie Otsuka that channels italic rhythm into upright, fixed-width letterforms, producing a syncopated visual pattern across three widths and seven weights.
description: Designed by Marie Otsuka and released in 2021, Pentameter reframes the constraints of the monospaced format as an expressive opportunity. The letterforms adopt an "upright italic" construction, generating movement and texture at the typographic level while the uniform metrics remain intact. Three width variants (Condensed, Regular and Wide) each span seven weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics, and the character set covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Pentameter as an exploration of the polyrhythmic potential that usually stays dormant inside monospaced typefaces. As an "upright italic," the letterforms create a lively pattern while their uniform metrics remain steady, resulting in a design on a syncopated beat that resonates with the poetics beyond code.

name: Prensa
classification_notes: Prensa draws on the tradition of 20th-century bookfaces, citing Dwiggins' Electra as a direct influence, with deliberate tension between outer and inner curves typical of transitional serif construction.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith built around deliberate tension between outer and inner curves, drawing direct influence from W.A. Dwiggins' 1935 bookface Electra.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and first released in 1999, Prensa applies a technique of wrapping outside curves around the inside to generate formal tension, most visible in the italics which depart from conventional forms in favour of a balance between simplicity and energy. The family spans six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, and includes a Display variant alongside a full set of small caps, oldstyle figures and ligatures. The broad Latin Extended character set makes it well-suited to multilingual editorial and book work.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa as a dynamic text series by Cyrus Highsmith whose character emerges from his process of wrapping outside curves around inside curves to create deliberate tension, a technique epitomised in Dwiggins' Electra. That tension is particularly evident in the italics, which move away from traditional forms to find a balance between simplicity and vitality. The family also includes Prensa Display.

name: Prensa Display
classification_notes: A display-optimised serif companion to Highsmith's Prensa text face, featuring sharper details and tighter spacing characteristic of high-contrast display serifs intended for headlines.
summary: A sharpened, tightly-fitted display companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Prensa, built for powerful headlines across three widths.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith as a companion to his Prensa text face, this display cut retains Prensa's characteristic tension in the letterforms whilst introducing sharper features and a more compact fit suited to large sizes. The family spans six weights from Light to Black with italics throughout, and extends into Condensed and Compressed widths for demanding headline settings. Small caps, oldstyle figures, ligatures and broad Latin Extended coverage make it a complete editorial tool.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa Display as sharing Prensa's distinctive tension within the letterforms but with sharper features and a more compact fit, and note that it includes additional narrow widths specifically for packing in powerful headlines.

name: Quiosco
classification_notes: Quiosco draws on Highsmith's study of W.A. Dwiggins, exploring contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms in a manner consistent with humanist serif traditions suited to text composition.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith built around Dwiggins-influenced contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms, engineered for narrow columns with tight wordspacing.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2007, Quiosco emerges from his sustained study of W.A. Dwiggins and the formal tension between a letter's interior and exterior contours. Three weights run from Regular to Bold, each with an italic, and the family includes small caps, old-style figures, tabular figures and a full suite of ligatures. It performs particularly well in narrow-column settings, where its lively texture and tolerance for compact wordspaces maintain readability without sacrifice.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Quiosco as a text family by Cyrus Highsmith that takes its name from the Spanish word for kiosk. They situate it within his broader investigation of W.A. Dwiggins's work and the contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms, noting that it brings lively texture to narrow columns and allows compact wordspaces without loss of readability.

name: Prensa
classification_notes: A text serif designed for editorial use, Prensa draws on the Dwiggins tradition of wrapping outside curves around inside curves to create tension, placing it in the lineage of mid-20th-century American bookfaces such as Electra.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith that applies the Dwiggins technique of opposing outside and inside curves to generate tension, most visible in its departures from conventional italic forms.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and first released in 1999, Prensa takes its name from the Spanish word for press and draws directly on the curve-tension method exemplified by W.A. Dwiggins's Electra of 1935. Six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, provide range for editorial hierarchies, while the italics deliberately stray from traditional forms to balance economy with vitality. A Display subfamily extends the family's use into larger settings.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa as a dynamic text series by Cyrus Highsmith, built through his technique of wrapping outside curves around the inside to create deliberate tension, a method they trace to Dwiggins's 1935 bookface Electra. The family's character, they note, is especially apparent in its italics, which depart from conventional forms in favour of simplicity and vitality.

name: Quiosco
classification_notes: Quiosco draws on the W.A. Dwiggins tradition, exploring contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms. Its lively texture and compact spacing mark it as a humanist text serif designed for narrow column settings.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith rooted in W.A. Dwiggins' approach to letterform contrast, built for narrow columns and compact wordspacing.
description: Designed in 2007, Quiosco explores the tension between the interior and exterior shapes of letterforms, a concern drawn directly from Highsmith's study of W.A. Dwiggins. Six styles across three weights from Regular to Bold include small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, and an extensive set of ligatures. The design holds its texture well at tight wordspaces, making it particularly useful for newspaper, magazine and book settings where column width is limited.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Quiosco as a text family by Cyrus Highsmith, its name the Spanish word for kiosk, and part of his ongoing exploration of Dwiggins' work and the contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms. The design brings lively texture to narrow columns and allows compact wordspaces without sacrificing readability.

name: Quiosco Display
classification_notes: A display-optimised reworking of the Quiosco text serif, redrawn with sharper, more chiselled forms suited to large headline sizes rather than continuous text.
summary: A headline-optimised redrawing of Cyrus Highsmith's Quiosco, recut by Cem Eskinazi with sharper, stone-carved forms and an extended weight range.
description: Cem Eskinazi reimagined Highsmith's clay-like Quiosco text serif into something harder-edged and lapidary, suited to large display sizes. Eight styles run from Regular to Black with matching italics, and the family adds a dedicated set of figures for use alongside the small caps. The result is a display serif with genuine character, well-suited to editorial headlines and magazine work.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Quiosco Display as Cem Eskinazi taking Quiosco and sharpening it for headlines, redrawing Highsmith's textface and imagining a transformation from something made of clay into something chiselled in stone, while extending the weight range and adding a special set of figures to accompany the small caps.

name: Quiosco Display
classification_notes: A sharpened, headline-optimised adaptation of the text serif Quiosco, redrawn with greater crispness and contrast for large-size use, consistent with a high-contrast display serif tradition.
summary: A headline-optimised redrawing of Highsmith's Quiosco, sharpened by Cem Eskinazi from clay-like text origins into something more precisely chiselled for large-scale use.
description: Where Quiosco was designed for text, this display companion was redrawn by Cem Eskinazi with crisper, more incisive forms suited to headline sizes. Eskinazi extended the weight range and added a dedicated set of figures to accompany the small caps. The result is a family of eight styles spanning Regular to Black with matching italics, carrying the character of the original text face into a more sharply defined register.
foundry_blurb: Cem Eskinazi took Quiosco and sharpened it for headlines, reimagining Highsmith's textface as something transformed from clay into chiselled stone. He extended the weight range and drew a special set of figures to accompany the small caps, releasing the family in 2019.

name: Relay
classification_notes: Highsmith describes it as his closest approach to a geometric sans, drawing on art deco, American sign lettering and modernist geometry, placing it firmly in the geometric tradition with humanist inflections.
summary: A geometric sans by Cyrus Highsmith that pulls from European art deco, American sign lettering and modernist geometry across five weights and four widths.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith and first released in 2001, Relay is the designer's nearest point to a pure geometric sans, though it picks up divergent influences along the way: European art deco letterforms, American sign-painting conventions and modernist structural logic sit in uneasy, productive tension. The family spans five weights from Light to Black across four widths — standard, Wide, Condensed and Compressed — each with matching italics, giving it considerable range across headline and text settings.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Relay as Cyrus Highsmith's closest approach to a geometric sans serif, noting that it zigs and zags between European art deco, American sign lettering and modernist geometry. It comes in five weights and three widths with matching italics, intended for setting headlines and text of all kinds.

name: Salvo Sans
classification_notes: Salvo Sans carries an energetic, optimistic character typical of humanist sans designs, with a warmth and personality suited to editorial and display use rather than the neutrality of a grotesque.
summary: A five-weight, three-width sans designed by Cyrus Highsmith to unify the typography of three separate magazines under a single family.
description: Originally commissioned to bring typographic coherence across three different magazines, Salvo Sans spans five weights from Light to Black, each with a corresponding italic, across standard, Condensed and Extra Condensed widths. The 30-style family covers headlines, pull quotes and short text, giving editorial designers considerable range within a single voice. It pairs with Salvo Serif as part of the broader Salvo series.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Salvo Sans as an energetic, optimistic design created by Cyrus Highsmith as part of the extensive Salvo series, originally conceived to unify three different magazines under one typographic umbrella. Available in five weights and three widths with corresponding italics, it works across headlines, pull quotes and short text.

name: Rapport
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans serif, with a wide, warm character rooted in 19th-century graphic sources rather than modernist reduction.
summary: A wide, warm grotesque designed by June Shin, originating from signage work for a 2017 RISD Museum exhibition and drawn from 19th-century etching frontispiece lettering.
description: Rapport grew out of exhibition design for Altered States at RISD Museum in 2017, with June Shin taking direct visual cues from the frontispiece of an 1862 collection of Charles François Daubigny's etchings. The result is a grotesque with conspicuous width and whimsical details that give it a friendly, slightly offbeat character unusual for the genre. Six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, are supported by small caps, old-style figures, tabular figures and a full suite of ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Rapport as a grotesque sans serif that exudes warmth, born on the walls of RISD Museum when June Shin designed the exhibition Altered States in 2017. She drew inspiration from the frontispiece of the 1862 publication of Charles François Daubigny's etchings, with the typeface's general wideness and whimsical features contributing to its quirky, friendly character.

name: Salvo Sans
classification_notes: Salvo Sans has an energetic, warm character typical of humanist sans designs, with broad language support and a construction that balances display and short-text use.
summary: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2011 to unify three magazines under one typographic system, spanning five weights and three widths with italics throughout.
description: Originally developed for a publishing client needing typographic consistency across multiple magazine titles, Salvo Sans covers thirty styles across regular, condensed and extra condensed widths. The five weights from Light to Black each carry a matching italic, giving editors flexibility across headlines, pull quotes and short passages of text. Its upbeat, open character makes it well suited to print contexts where warmth and legibility need to coexist.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Salvo Sans as an energetic and optimistic design created by Cyrus Highsmith as part of the broader Salvo series, which was developed to bring typographic unity across three different magazines. The family offers five weights and three widths with corresponding italics, handling headlines, pull quotes and short text settings.

name: Salvo Serif
classification_notes: Heavy bracketed serifs with an assertive, high-contrast structure place this firmly in the slab category. The outspoken weight and wide range of widths give it a display-leaning character suited to editorial work.
summary: A slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith designed to unify three different magazines under a single typographic system, spanning five weights and three widths with italics throughout.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2011, Salvo Serif was conceived as part of the broader Salvo series, built to bring typographic coherence across multiple magazine titles. The family runs across five weights from Light to Black in three widths — regular, condensed and extra condensed — each with matching italics, totalling 30 styles. Heavy serifs work to bind letters into coherent word shapes, which draws the reader's eye and keeps text legible at editorial scales.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Salvo Serif as an outspoken slab design created by Cyrus Highsmith to help unify three different magazines under one typographic umbrella. The heavy serifs form unified words that catch the reader's eye and draw them into the text, offered in five weights and three widths with corresponding italics.

name: Salvo Serif
classification_notes: Salvo Serif features heavy, prominent serifs that aid word cohesion and legibility, placing it firmly in the slab serif category. Its outspoken character and magazine origins align it with a text-focused workhorse slab tradition.
