summary: A slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith designed to unify three magazines under a single typographic system, available in five weights and three widths with italics throughout.
description: Commissioned to bring typographic coherence across three different magazine titles, Salvo Serif's heavy serifs are used deliberately to bind letterforms into readable words that attract the eye at editorial scale. The family spans five weights from Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Extra Condensed widths, each with a matching italic, totalling 30 styles. Designed in 2011 by Cyrus Highsmith, it pairs with the companion Salvo Sans for mixed-setting across editorial and magazine contexts.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith created the Salvo series to provide typographic unity across three magazines. Salvo Serif is described as an outspoken slab design offered in five weights and three widths with corresponding italics, with heavy serifs that form unified words to draw readers into the text.

name: Scout Text
classification_notes: Scout Text is a text-optimised companion to Scout, a utilitarian sans serif. Its increased x-height, open spacing and added legibility features place it firmly in the functional sans-serif tradition designed for small-size setting.
summary: A text-optimised companion to Scout, adjusted by Cyrus Highsmith for small-size legibility with a raised x-height, open spacing and distinctive tail details on the 'l' and 'a'.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith to complement his utilitarian sans Scout, this variant trades Scout's standard proportions for a taller x-height and more open spacing suited to small sizes. Tails added to the lowercase 'l' and 'a' further distinguish letterforms under demanding conditions. Four weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, cover most text-setting needs.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Scout Text as a companion to the popular utilitarian sans Scout, optimised by Cyrus Highsmith for small sizes through an increased x-height, open spacing and the addition of tails to the lowercase 'l' and 'a'. It comes in four weights plus italics.

name: Scout Text
classification_notes: Scout Text is a humanist sans serif, companion to the utilitarian Scout, with legibility-focused modifications such as raised x-height, open spacing and disambiguating tails on the lowercase 'l' and 'a' for small-size use.
summary: A text-optimised companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Scout, redrawn for small sizes with a taller x-height, open spacing and disambiguating details on the 'l' and 'a'.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith in 2016 as a legibility-focused counterpart to his utilitarian Scout sans serif, Scout Text introduces a taller x-height, looser spacing and tails on the lowercase 'l' and 'a' to reduce confusion at small sizes. It comes in four weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, giving editors and designers a practical range for body text and caption work. The extended Latin character set covers a wide range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Scout Text as a companion to Cyrus Highsmith's popular utilitarian sans serif Scout, optimised for small sizes through an increased x-height, more open spacing and added tails to the lowercase 'l' and 'a' for improved legibility. It is available in four weights with italics.

name: Scout
classification_notes: Scout draws directly from the DIN 1451 tradition, blending it with references to Venus and Cairoli to produce a geometric-industrial grotesque with more character than a strict DIN revival.
summary: An industrial grotesque by Cyrus Highsmith that takes DIN 1451 as a starting point, then pulls in Venus and Cairoli to shift it toward something more considered and typographically complete.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith with Greek and Cyrillic alphabets added by June Shin, Scout runs across six weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, in both standard and condensed widths. The explicit brief was to satisfy the demand for a DIN-like sans without being a straight revival, and the admixture of Venus and Cairoli gives it a warmer rhythm suited to headlines, captions and sidebars alike. Script coverage across Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek makes it a practical choice for multilingual editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith drew Scout in response to the recurring demand for an industrial sans that recalls DIN 1451 without copying it, mixing in influences from Venus and Cairoli alongside his own graphic sensibilities. It covers a wide weight range in two widths plus italics, with June Shin contributing the Greek and Cyrillic alphabets.

name: Serge
classification_notes: Serge is described as an acrobatic, decorative design intended for headlines, signs and blurbs rather than text setting, with swash caps and an expressive character that places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A three-weight display serif by Cyrus Highsmith, built for headlines and decorative use, with swash capitals adding an extra flourish.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2012, Serge is a departure from his utilitarian workhorse families, pitched instead at decorative blurbs, signage and headline work. Three weights run from Regular to Black, each retaining an agile, expressive quality rather than gaining solidity as the weight increases. A set of swash caps extends the family's ornamental range without tipping into excess.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Serge as Cyrus Highsmith letting loose after his all-round workhorse families like Antenna, Scout and Salvo. They characterise it as an acrobatic design for decorative blurbs, signs and headlines, spanning three weights from Regular to Black, all light on their feet, with swash caps providing a subtle flair.

name: Scout
classification_notes: Drawn as a DIN-adjacent industrial sans, Scout takes clear cues from DIN 1451 while incorporating influences from Venus and Cairoli, placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition with an industrial character.
summary: Cyrus Highsmith's DIN-influenced industrial grotesque, drawing on Venus and Cairoli, available across six weights in two widths with italics and extensive Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2018, Scout answers the recurring demand for a DIN-like industrial sans that doesn't replicate DIN 1451 directly, blending in proportions and details from Venus and Cairoli. The family spans six weights from Thin to Black in both regular and condensed widths, each with a matching italic, making it practical for headlines, captions and sidebars alike. June Shin extended the character set to include Greek and Cyrillic, broadening its reach considerably beyond Latin-only alternatives.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Scout as Cyrus Highsmith's response to persistent requests for an industrial sans in the vein of DIN 1451, but with its own identity. He drew on Venus and Cairoli alongside his own graphic instincts, producing a family with a wide range of weights and two widths plus italics suited to editorial applications. Greek and Cyrillic were added by June Shin.

name: Stainless 2
classification_notes: Stainless 2 is a sans-serif derived from a slab serif (Dispatch), stripped of serifs to produce a clean grotesque character. Its broad width range and editorial application place it firmly in the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans derived from Cyrus Highsmith's slab serif Dispatch, expanded into a 50-style family spanning five widths and five weights with italics throughout.
description: Originally an experiment branching off Highsmith's Dispatch, Stainless has earned a track record in editorial headlines and pull quotes across publications from Señor Fútbol to the Wall Street Journal. The re-release as Stainless 2, assisted by Cem Eskinazi, adds Cyrillic and Greek alongside a deep Latin Extended character set, and organises the family into Compressed, Condensed, Regular, Extended and Wide subfamilies. That range of widths makes it adaptable across tight column measures and large-format display work without changing typeface.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Stainless as derived from Cyrus Highsmith's slab serif Dispatch, beginning as a simple experiment before evolving into its own series. It has appeared in headlines and pull quotes in publications worldwide, and was re-released as Stainless 2 with assistance from Cem Eskinazi.

name: Tock
classification_notes: Tock is a modular stencil display face built from a mechanical kit of parts, drawing on Bauhaus geometric principles and industrial lettering traditions. Its single-weight, constructed letterforms place it firmly in the display category with a stencil-geometric subcategory.
summary: A mechanised, modular stencil face by Cyrus Highsmith that pairs with its companion Tick, suited to industrial and Bauhaus-influenced display work.
description: Built from the same kit of parts as Tick, Tock pushes the system toward a harder, more mechanical character. It comes in a single weight, with no italic or condensed variants, keeping it focused as a display tool for bold, constructed typography. Coverage spans extended Latin with fractions, ligatures and superior and inferior figures included.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Tock as the mechanised counterpart to Tick, both reflecting Cyrus Highsmith's long interest in stencils and modular alphabets. They suggest it would sit naturally on a Bauhaus poster or the side of a bulldozer, and note that like Tick it comes in just one mighty weight.

name: Zócalo Banner
classification_notes: Inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle letterforms and designed for headline use, Zócalo Banner is a display-oriented oldstyle serif with simplified serifs adapted for larger sizes such as newspaper headlines and decks.
summary: A headline-weight oldstyle serif by Cyrus Highsmith, designed for newspaper display use with simplified serifs drawn from Nicholas Kis's oldstyle tradition.
description: Developed as part of Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of the Mexico City newspaper El Universal, Zócalo Banner is the headline and deck variant of Zócalo Display, distinguished by its simplified serifs suited to larger sizes. Cyrus Highsmith drew on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle forms alongside his own impressions of México, giving the family a character rooted in both European type history and Latin American newspaper culture. Four weights from Regular to Black, each with matching italics, make it a considered choice for editorial hierarchies where warmth and historical depth matter.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Banner as a stylistic variation of Zócalo Display, with simplified serifs intended for headlines and decks. The Zócalo series was designed by Cyrus Highsmith as part of Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, Mexico City's leading newspaper, and was inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle as well as Highsmith's impressions of México itself. Zócalo Banner was released in 2005.

name: Tick
classification_notes: Tick is a single-weight stencil display face derived from paper-cutting and modular alphabet construction, with a randomising feature that simulates handmade irregularity. Its primary function is large-scale attention-grabbing text rather than extended setting.
summary: A single-weight stencil display face by Cyrus Highsmith, born from paper-cutting and featuring a randomising function that lends words a handmade, irregular quality.
description: Drawn from Highsmith's interest in stencils and modular alphabets, Tick uses a randomising feature built into the font to vary letterforms across a word, avoiding the mechanical repetition common to stencil types. Available in one weight only, it covers Latin Extended with stylistic alternates for key uppercase letters and a full set of ligatures. Suited to short display use where a rough, cut-paper texture is the point.
foundry_blurb: Tick and Tock reflect Cyrus Highsmith's longstanding interest in stencils and modular alphabets. Tick originated in paper-cutting and includes a randomising feature that gives words a natural, handmade appearance. It comes in one weight, designed for attracting attention at display sizes.

name: Zócalo Display
classification_notes: Inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle letterforms and designed for headline use, Zócalo Display belongs firmly in the oldstyle serif tradition with its compact, display-optimised proportions.
summary: A compact display serif by Cyrus Highsmith, rooted in Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and shaped by his experience of México, originally drawn for the 2002 redesign of El Universal.
description: Designed in 2005 as the headline and deck weight of the Zócalo series, this face carries the optical sparkle and tight spacing suited to large sizes and newspaper front pages. Its origins in Eduardo Danilo's redesign of the Mexico City broadsheet El Universal give it a clear editorial pedigree, whilst the Kis-derived oldstyle structure lends it historical weight. Part of a three-member family alongside Zócalo Text and Zócalo Banner, each optimised for a different size range.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Display as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, which was created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, the leading Mexico City newspaper. The design draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and on Highsmith's own impressions of México, resulting in a sparkling, compact face intended for headlines and decks.

name: Zócalo Display
classification_notes: Inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle letterforms, with a compact, high-contrast cut optimised for display use, placing it firmly in the oldstyle serif tradition adapted for headline setting.
summary: A compact oldstyle serif by Cyrus Highsmith, designed for headlines and decks as part of the 2002 redesign of Mexico City's El Universal.
description: Born out of Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, one of Mexico City's principal newspapers, this display cut draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle models and Highsmith's direct impressions of México. The family spans four weights from Regular to Black, each with an italic, and sits alongside sibling families Zócalo Text and Zócalo Banner to form a complete editorial system. The sparkling, compact proportions make it well-suited to tight headline measures and deck copy.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Display as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, originally created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, the leading Mexico City newspaper. Inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and by Highsmith's impressions of México itself, Zócalo Display is characterised as a sparkling, compact design for headlines and decks.

name: Zócalo Text
classification_notes: Zócalo Text draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle tradition and Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5 newsface, placing it firmly in the oldstyle serif category with robust, text-optimised proportions suited to newspaper and editorial work.
summary: A sturdy text serif by Cyrus Highsmith, originally cut for the 2002 redesign of Mexico City's El Universal and drawing on Ionic No.5 and Nicholas Kis's oldstyle tradition.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2006, Zócalo Text was born from Eduardo Danilo's redesign of El Universal, one of Mexico City's leading newspapers. Its proportions are grounded in Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5, a classic newsface built for legibility under pressure, while the letterforms also reflect Nicholas Kis's oldstyle influence and Highsmith's own impressions of México. The family ships in four styles with small caps, oldstyle and tabular figures, and ligatures, making it a thorough tool for demanding text settings.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Text as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, originally created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal in Mexico City. The typeface draws inspiration from Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's impressions of México, with its sturdy proportions derived from Chauncey Griffith's classic newsface Ionic No.5.

name: Zócalo Banner
classification_notes: Zócalo Banner is rooted in the oldstyle tradition, inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle letterforms, with simplified serifs optimised for headline and deck sizes. Its origins in newspaper typography reinforce its classification as a workhorse oldstyle serif.
summary: A headline-weight oldstyle serif by Cyrus Highsmith, designed as part of a newspaper family for El Universal's 2002 redesign, with simplified serifs suited to large display settings.
description: Zócalo Banner is one of three optical variants in Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, which was commissioned for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of Mexico City's El Universal newspaper. It draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle forms, filtered through Highsmith's impressions of México, and simplifies the serifs of Zócalo Display to perform at headline and deck sizes. Four weights from Regular to Black, each with an italic, make it a versatile choice for editorial and newspaper contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Banner as a stylistic variation of Zócalo Display, part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, Mexico City's leading newspaper. The design was inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's own impressions of México, with Banner specifically featuring simplified serifs for use in headlines and decks.

name: Zócalo Text
classification_notes: Rooted in oldstyle influences from Nicholas Kis and the sturdy newsface proportions of Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5, Zócalo Text sits at the intersection of oldstyle and transitional newspaper serif traditions.
summary: A newspaper text serif by Cyrus Highsmith, designed for the 2002 redesign of El Universal, drawing on Kis's oldstyle and Griffith's Ionic No.5 for its sturdy proportions.
description: Commissioned as part of Eduardo Danilo's redesign of the leading Mexico City daily El Universal, Zócalo Text takes its structural weight and density from Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5, the classic American newsface, while its letterform character draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's impressions of México. The family is spare at four styles, Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic, but is generously appointed with small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and a full set of ligatures. It sits within a wider Zócalo series that includes Display and Banner cuts for contrasting use at larger sizes.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Text as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, the leading Mexico City newspaper. The text cut draws its sturdy proportions from Chauncey Griffith's classic newsface Ionic No.5, while the broader series was inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's impressions of México itself. Released in 2006.
